feed.title: Combined summary - Compact Isogeny PQC can replace HD wallets, key-tweaking, silent payments

entry.title: Combined summary - Compact Isogeny PQC can replace HD wallets, key-tweaking, silent payments
entry.summary: The recent discourse on post-quantum cryptography highlights a shift towards exploring alternatives to lattice-based cryptographic schemes due to their bulky key and signature sizes. Among the promising alternatives, isogeny-based cryptography stands out for its compactness, with keys and signatures totaling less than 300 bytes, and its error-free operation. Isogenies represent mappings between elliptic curves, offering a new approach to encryption that could potentially safeguard against quantum computer attacks. The concept of endomorphisms, or isogenies mapping points within the same curve, plays a crucial role in understanding this cryptographic method. A significant challenge in isogeny cryptography is the supersingular isogeny path problem (SIPP), which posits that finding an isogeny between two given curves is computationally difficult. This problem is intrinsically linked to the difficulty of determining the endomorphism ring of a curve, a mathematical structure that encompasses all possible endomorphisms for a given curve. The equivalence between solving the SIPP and finding the endomorphism ring (ERP) lays the foundation for generating secure cryptographic keys. Specifically, knowing the endomorphism rings of two curves enables the efficient computation of an isogeny between them, facilitating the creation of secret keys based on these mathematical relationships. Isogeny-based cryptography not only offers a pathway to secure communication in the quantum era but also introduces innovative functionalities such as unhardened BIP32 key derivation, silent payments, and taproot-style key tweaking. These features are achieved through the generation of pseudorandom isogenies, mapping public keys to new curves, and leveraging the well-understood properties of endomorphism rings. Despite these advancements, the development of compact multisignature schemes that can compete with existing classical ECC-based systems remains an area ripe for further research. Engagement from the broader bitcoin development community is crucial in advancing isogeny cryptography. By delving into the nuances of this technology, developers can contribute to the evolution of cryptographic schemes that ensure security against quantum attacks while maintaining the efficiency and functionality critical for widespread adoption. Furthermore, the extension of cryptographic concepts such as zero-knowledge proofs of knowledge (zkpok) to isogeny-based systems indicates a broader applicability of these principles beyond traditional elliptic curve cryptography (ECC). The exploration of how one-way functions and homomorphic properties translate into the realm of isogenies reveals both challenges and opportunities for innovation. The dialogue around these cryptographic primitives underscores the importance of considering properties like rerandomization, aggregatability, and batch verifiability in the design of post-quantum algorithms. As the industry gravitates towards hash-based signatures for their simplicity and proven security, the unique advantages of isogeny-based cryptography warrant further investigation to ensure a diverse and robust cryptographic landscape in the face of evolving technological threats.
